bio: Joyce Kathryn Roorda, later Joyce Kathryn Lourens, was born in Pella and grew up attending Pleasant Grove and Maple Grove country schools before graduating from Prairie City High School in 1957. After high school she worked for the State Health Department, then focused on raising her sons, Douglas and Kevin. In 1978, when Adventure-Life Reformed Church was organized, she became its secretary and served there for 26 years before retirement.
funFacts: She and Larry Lourens were charter members of Adventure-Life Reformed Church.
Her obituary notes long volunteer service at Caring Hands Food Pantry in Altoona.
Her hobbies included reading, scrapbooking, embroidering, camping, and traveling.
Her obituary calls her the only child of Edward and Minnie Roorda, preserving Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda's family nickname.
records: Hamilton's Funeral Home obituary
The Oskaloosa Herald obituary

citations.label: Hamilton's Funeral Home obituary: Joyce Kathryn Lourens
The Oskaloosa Herald obituary: Joyce Kathryn Lourens

bio: Epp R. Roorda was born near Pella, Iowa, to John E. and Adriana Van Maaren Roorda and married Cornelia Vander Wilt in 1897. His Pella Chronicle obituary says he and Cornelia had seven children, and that he was survived by a very large family network of grandchildren and great-grandchildren. He spent his last decade at the Pella Rest Home and died at Pella Community Hospital at age 91.
funFacts: He was a member of First Reformed Church in Pella for about 40 years.
His obituary says he served on the church consistory for a number of years.
At his death he was survived by seven children, 19 grandchildren, 3 step-grandchildren, and 44 great-grandchildren.
Oakwood Cemetery recorded his sons by everyday names: Dave, Robert, Ed, and Henry.
records: Pella Chronicle obituary transcribed on Find a Grave
Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Pella, Iowa

citations.label: Find a Grave memorial with Pella Chronicle obituary: Epp Robert Roorda
IAGenWeb Marion County: Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Part B

bio: Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda was born north of Pella, daughter of John Vander Wilt and Gora/Govertje Den Burger. She married Epp R. Roorda on 10 February 1897, and their obituary record names seven living children at her death in 1949. She lived at 1116 Marion Street in Pella at the time of her death.
funFacts: Her obituary says she had 19 grandchildren and 4 great-grandchildren by 1949.
She had a twin sister, Sarah, and the obituary names one brother and nine sisters who predeceased her.
She was described as a faithful First Reformed Church member who attended worship until lingering illness prevented it.
Oakwood Cemetery records her as Mrs. Epp Roorda, née Vander Wilt, buried in the same lot 10 as Epp R. Roorda.
records: Pella Chronicle obituary transcribed on Find a Grave
Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Pella, Iowa

IAGenWeb Marion County: Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Part B

bio: Ruurd, also called John, Epkes Roorda was the youngest child of Epke Alberts Roorda and Froukje Wiegers van der Lune. The family history in IAGenWeb's translation of Greetings from America says he came to America with his parents in 1857 and later married Adrianna Jane van Maren in Pella in 1868. He farmed in the Durham neighborhood and also peddled housewares, eggs, and vegetables door to door.
funFacts: He celebrated his 11th birthday aboard ship on the family's 1857 journey to America.
He and Adrianna Jane van Maren had 12 children: 10 sons and 2 daughters.
His siblings' immigration was staggered: three preceded the parents to America, four traveled with the parents, and two stayed in the Netherlands.
His Pella Chronicle obituary, transcribed on Find a Grave, says he farmed south of the Des Moines River before retiring to Pella.
That obituary says he died at home after a three-week illness and was buried at Eureka Cemetery near Durham.
immigration: Immigrated from Dantumawoude, Friesland, to Pella, Iowa, in 1857 with his parents and siblings Hendrik, Gerrit, and Hinke.
records: IAGenWeb translation of Greetings from America, Chapter 9
Find a Grave memorial with Pella Chronicle obituary

citations.label: IAGenWeb: Greetings from America, Chapter 9, Family of Epke Alberts Roorda
Find a Grave memorial with Pella Chronicle obituary: Ruurt Epke "John" Roorda

bio: Adrianna Jane van Maren married Ruurd (John) Epkes Roorda on 7 October 1868 in Pella. The family history identifies her as the daughter of Maren and Antje van der Linden and says she was born on 23 September 1849, possibly in Friesland. She and John raised a large Roorda family in the Pella area.
funFacts: The family history lists 12 children for Adrianna and John: Henry, John, Epp, Wieger, Luke, Anna, Andrew, Albert, Izaak, Anne, Gerrit, and Janie.
Her husband's work combined farming near Durham with peddling housewares, eggs, and vegetables door to door.
records: IAGenWeb translation of Greetings from America, Chapter 9

citations.label: IAGenWeb: Greetings from America, Chapter 9, Family of Epke Alberts Roorda

bio: Jan Vander Wilt appears to match the younger Jan listed in the van der Wilt household on the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger list transcribed in the WikiTree Pella immigrant project. The household included Gerrit van der Wilt, Sophia Spruit, several older children with farmer occupations, and Jan, age 15, also listed as a farmer. This places him among the Dutch immigrant families who followed the early Pella settlement stream.
funFacts: The same passenger-list household included multiple working-age van der Wilt sons identified as farmers.
His daughter Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda's obituary says Cornelia was born north of Pella and identifies her parents as John Vander Wilt and Gora/Govertje Den Burger.
immigration: Likely immigrated in 1847 on the Pieter Floris as part of a van der Wilt household from the Netherlands to Baltimore, then onward to the Pella, Iowa settlement.
records: WikiTree Pella immigrant passenger-list transcription
Pella Chronicle obituary for daughter Cornelia

Find a Grave memorial with Pella Chronicle obituary: Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda

bio: Govertje Den Burger appears in the den Burger household on the 1847 Maasstroom passenger list transcribed for the WikiTree Pella immigrant project. She was listed as age 11 in the household of Jan den Burger, alongside Mantje and several older children. In Iowa records she is represented in her daughter Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda's obituary as Gora/Govertje Den Burger, wife of John Vander Wilt.
funFacts: She was still a child when the den Burger family crossed the Atlantic in 1847.
Her daughter Cornelia's obituary shows the family settled north of Pella and became tied into the Roorda line through Cornelia's marriage to Epp R. Roorda.
immigration: Listed as an 11-year-old in the den Burger household on the 1847 Maasstroom passenger list associated with the Dutch migration to Pella, Iowa.
records: WikiTree Pella immigrant passenger-list transcription
Pella Chronicle obituary for daughter Cornelia

bio: Klaas Hendrik de Fockert belongs to the Herwijnen De Fockert/Hackert line documented in the Pella Community Memory Database's thirty-one-page Dutch register of the family. A page of that register names Klaas Hendrik de Fockert, born at Herwijnen on 20 December 1840, and records his marriage at Herwijnen on 15 March 1867 to Cornelia Hakkert/Rakkert. The same register continues with their children, including Pieter de Fockert, born at Herwijnen on 5 February 1880.
funFacts: The Pella Community Memory Database describes the De Fockert register as covering the family in Herwijnen from 1601 to 1942 and including a family crest.
PCMD metadata for a related Hackert family file notes that the surname Hackert was previously known as DeFockert.
records: Pella Community Memory Database: Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen

citations.label: PCMD: Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen
PCMD: De Fockert register, page naming Klaas Hendrik and Cornelia
PCMD search result: Hackert surname formerly DeFockert

bio: Cornelia Hakkert/Rakkert is named in the Herwijnen De Fockert family register as the wife of Klaas Hendrik de Fockert. The register page says Klaas Hendrik married her at Herwijnen on 15 March 1867 and identifies her as the daughter of Klaas and Maria van Zee, born at Herwijnen on 15 October 1845. Their children are continued in the register, tying the Dutch De Fockert/Hackert family to the later Pella Hackert line.
funFacts: Her son Pieter de Fockert, born 5 February 1880 at Herwijnen, matches the Peter Hackert later found in the Pella branch.
The source preserves the older Dutch family form De Fockert beside the later Hackert spelling.
records: Pella Community Memory Database: Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen

citations.label: PCMD: De Fockert register, page naming Klaas Hendrik and Cornelia
PCMD: De Fockert register, children of Klaas Hendrik and Cornelia

bio: Peter Hackert matches Pieter de Fockert in the Herwijnen De Fockert register: the page for children of Klaas Hendrik de Fockert and Cornelia Rakkert lists Pieter de Fockert born at Herwijnen on 5 February 1880. In Pella records, his son Henry Hackert's obituary identifies Henry as the son of Peter and Johanna (De Wild) Hackert and says Henry was born in Pella in 1916. This links Peter's Dutch birth family with the Hackert household south of Pella.
funFacts: His son Henry lived most of his life in the same home south of Pella and worked on the farm all his life, according to Henry's obituary.
Henry Hackert later combined farming with 18 years as a rural mail carrier for the United States Postal Service.
records: Pella Community Memory Database De Fockert register
IAGenWeb obituary for son Henry Hackert

citations.label: PCMD: De Fockert register, children of Klaas Hendrik and Cornelia
IAGenWeb Marion County obituary: Henry Hackert

bio: Johanna DeWild appears in Pella records as Johanna (De Wild) Hackert, wife of Peter Hackert and mother of Henry Hackert. Henry's obituary says he was born in Pella on 23 February 1916, son of Peter and Johanna (De Wild) Hackert, and that he lived most of his life in the same home south of Pella. Through daughter Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda, Johanna's line connects directly into the Roorda/Lourens branch.
funFacts: Henry Hackert's obituary names his sisters Cornelia Schakel and Wilhelmina Roorda and brother Clarence Hackert, confirming several children of Peter and Johanna.
The obituary places the family in the Third Reformed Church community in Pella through Henry's funeral and membership.
records: IAGenWeb obituary for son Henry Hackert

citations.label: IAGenWeb Marion County obituary: Henry Hackert

bio: Epke Alberts Roorda was born at Rinsumageest in Friesland and married Froukje Wiegers van der Lune at Ferwerd in 1822. The family history says he was a painter and glassmaker, later kept a boarding house, and resumed painting after settling in Marion County, Iowa. He and Froukje emigrated from Dantumawoude to Pella in 1857 with four children, while three children had already gone to America and two remained in the Netherlands.
funFacts: Epke painted landscapes on glass in his spare time after arriving in America.
He and Froukje had 11 children, two of whom died young in Dantumawoude.
According to the History of Pella tradition quoted by IAGenWeb, the couple brought plum trees, rhubarb, roses, and other plants to America.
Their son Hendrik served in Company H, 17th Iowa Volunteers, and died during the Civil War at Corinth, Mississippi.
His 1822 civil marriage record calls him a 23-year-old "verver" from Rinsumageest, matching the family tradition that he worked as a painter.
A Ferwerderadeel notarial deed from November 1822 lists him at Damwoude as a debtor for capital of 1,000 guilders.
immigration: Left Dantumawoude for Pella, Iowa, in 1857 with wife Froukje and children Hendrik, Gerrit, Hinke, and Ruurd/John.
records: IAGenWeb translation of Greetings from America, Chapter 9
Find a Grave memorial biography

bio: Froukje Wiegers van der Lune was born at Hallum, Ferwerderadeel, daughter of Wieger Pieters van der Lune and Antje Gerrits Kooi. She married Epke Alberts Roorda at Ferwerd in 1822 and was matriarch of a large Roorda family that moved from Friesland to Pella, Iowa. The family history emphasizes that she and Epke were garden lovers as well as immigrants, carrying plants and family traditions into the Dutch community at Pella.
funFacts: The couple had 11 children, and two died young before the family emigrated.
Their family reportedly brought plum trees, rhubarb, roses, and other plants to America.
A PCMD photograph identifies Mr. and Mrs. Epke Alberts Roorda and notes their 30 May 1822 marriage.
Her 1822 civil marriage record lists her as a 21-year-old bride born in Hallum.
The same marriage record names her parents as Wieger Pieters van der Lune and Antje Gerrits Kooi, tying the Hallum family into the later Roorda immigrant line.
immigration: Immigrated from Friesland to Pella, Iowa, in 1857 with Epke and four of their children; other children had already preceded them to America.
records: IAGenWeb translation of Greetings from America, Chapter 9
Pella Community Memory Database photograph

bio: Jan van Maren appears in the Pieter Floris passenger-list transcription as Jan van Maren, age 37, farmer, traveling with Areke nee van der Linden and children Hendrik, Lucas, and Adriana. The family belonged to the 1847 Dutch immigrant stream connected with the founding generation of Pella, Iowa. His daughter Adrianna Jane van Maren later married Ruurd/John Epkes Roorda, joining the van Maren and Roorda lines in Marion County.
funFacts: The Pieter Floris list places Jan's household among many Dutch farm families bound for the Pella settlement.
The IAGenWeb Roorda family history names Adrianna Jane van Maren as John Roorda's wife and connects her to the van Maren/van der Linden family.
immigration: Listed as a 37-year-old farmer on the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger list for Dutch immigrants who settled in Pella, Iowa.
records: WikiTree Pella immigrant passenger-list transcription
IAGenWeb Roorda family history

bio: Areke van der Linden appears in the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger-list transcription as Areke, nee van der Linden, age 41, housewife, traveling with Jan van Maren and children Hendrik, Lucas, and Adriana. This places her in the Dutch migration stream that founded and populated Pella, Iowa. Her van Maren household later connected to the Roorda line through Adrianna Jane van Maren's marriage to Ruurd/John Epkes Roorda.
funFacts: The passenger-list entry preserves her maiden name directly as van der Linden.
Her household traveled with many other Dutch families whose occupations were heavily agricultural, reflecting the farming character of the early Pella colony.
immigration: Listed as age 41 in Jan van Maren's household on the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger list associated with the Dutch settlement at Pella, Iowa.
records: WikiTree Pella immigrant passenger-list transcription

citations.label: WikiTree: Dutch immigrants who settled in Pella, Pieter Floris passenger list

bio: Jan den Burger appears in the Maasstroom passenger-list transcription as Jan, age 55, traveling with Mantje and children including Cornelis, Arie, Joost, Patentje, Govertje, and Jan. The older sons Cornelis, Arie, and Joost are listed as farmers, suggesting the family joined the agrarian Dutch settler stream to Iowa. His daughter Govertje/Grace later married into the Vander Wilt family and became an ancestor of Cornelia Vander Wilt Roorda.
funFacts: The den Burger family was part of the 1847 Dutch immigrant movement associated with Hendrik Pieter Scholte's Pella settlement.
Several older children in the passenger-list household were already working-age farmers.
immigration: Listed at age 55 in the den Burger household on the 1847 Maasstroom passenger list connected with Dutch immigrants to Pella, Iowa.
records: WikiTree Pella immigrant passenger-list transcription

citations.label: WikiTree: Dutch immigrants who settled in Pella, Maasstroom passenger list

bio: Oakwood Cemetery records Edward C. Roorda with a calculated birth date of 14 September 1910, death date of 30 May 2003, and burial in lot 58. The same interment record lists his nearest relative as daughter Joyce; Joyce Lourens's obituary identifies her as the only child of Edward and Minnie Roorda.
funFacts: Oakwood Cemetery recorded his nearest relative simply as "Dau - Joyce," matching Joyce Lourens's obituary statement that she was Edward and Minnie Roorda's only child.
He and Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda are recorded in the same Oakwood Cemetery lot, lot 58.
His father Epp R. Roorda's Oakwood interment record names a son "Ed," suggesting Edward used the short family name Ed.
records: Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Pella, Iowa
Hamilton's Funeral Home obituary for daughter Joyce Kathryn Lourens

bio: Oakwood Cemetery records Wilhelmina Hackert Roorda as Mrs. Edward Roorda, née Wilhelmina Hackert, with a calculated birth date of 29 June 1912, death date of 21 December 1952, and burial in lot 58 with Edward. Her daughter Joyce's obituary preserves her family nickname, Minnie.
funFacts: Joyce Lourens's obituary preserves Wilhelmina's everyday family name as "Minnie."
Oakwood Cemetery calculated her age at death as 40 years, 5 months, and 22 days.
Her brother Henry Hackert's obituary names Wilhelmina Roorda among his sisters, alongside Cornelia Schakel and brother Clarence Hackert.
records: Oakwood Cemetery interment database, Pella, Iowa
Henry Hackert obituary on IAGenWeb

bio: Hinke Izaaks Roorda lived into the civil-registration era in Friesland: her 1837 Dantumawoude death record lists her as an 83-year-old widow and names her parents as Izaak Rinses and Jitske Ruurds. A Reformed baptism index from Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis records the baptism of Hinke, daughter of IJzak Rinses Roorda, on 21 April 1754.
funFacts: Her death record preserves both her age, 83, and widow status in Dantumawoude.
Her baptism is indexed in the Reformed church book for Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis, the same small Frisian church landscape carried through the Roorda line.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Dantumadeel civil death record, 1837
AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven DTB baptism record, Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis, 1754

bio: Wieger Pieters van der Lune appears in the Napoleonic-era Registres civiques for Ferwerderadeel as a Hallum resident born 2 October 1770 and working as a voerman/charretier, a carter or wagoner. His 1846 death record gives his age as 76 and names his parents as Pieter Fransen and Aukje Jans.
funFacts: The 1811-1812 civic register gives his occupation bilingually as "voerman / charretier," a wagoner or carter moving goods by horse and cart.
His civic-register entry places him at Hallum, the village where his daughter Froukje was born.
His death record says he was 76 when he died in Ferwerderadeel in November 1846.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Registres civiques entry, Ferwerderadeel
AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven civil death record, Ferwerderadeel, 1846

citations.label: OpenArchieven/AlleFriezen: Registres civiques entry for Wieger Pieters van der Lune
OpenArchieven/AlleFriezen: death of Wieger Pieters van de Lune, 1846

bio: IJzak Rinses Roorda is named in the Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis Reformed baptism book as the father of Hinke, baptized 21 April 1754. That church-book entry anchors the patronymic Roorda line in Dantumadeel before modern civil registration.
funFacts: The baptism index uses the spelling "IJzak Rinses Roorda," showing how the name could shift between IJzak, Izaak, and Ijzak in records.
The record places the family in the Hervormde gemeente Rinsumageest en Sijbrandahuis baptism register.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven DTB baptism record, Rinsumageest and Sijbrandahuis, 1754

citations.label: OpenArchieven/AlleFriezen: baptism of Hinke, daughter of IJzak Rinses Roorda, 1754

bio: Rinse Johan Roorda is the earliest Roorda ancestor named in this roster. The broader surname has a documented Frisian noble-family context around Roorda State at Genum, but the available source does not prove that this specific Rinse Johan descends from the Genum, Tzummarum, or Cubaard noble branches.
funFacts: Stinsen in Friesland describes Roorda as an important Frisian family with branches at Tzummarum, Cubaard, and Genum, but says it is difficult to show how those branches connect.
The same source says the Genum Roordas appear in records by 1397 as "Rowertha to Ghenim"; treat this as surname context, not proven ancestry for this tree line.
records: Stinsen in Friesland article on Roorda State at Genum

citations.label: Stinsen in Friesland: Roorda State at Genum

bio: Gerrit van der Wilt appears in the 1847 Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions as a 52-year-old farmer traveling with Sophia, née Spruit, and a large household of children. The list places the family among the Dutch emigrants connected with the early Pella, Iowa settlement movement. This is a passenger-list identification rather than a full biography, but it is a strong match for the Gerrit Vander Wilt household in this branch.
funFacts: The detailed WikiTree transcription lists two sons named Jan in the household, ages 24 and 15, while the IAGenWeb transcription distinguishes them as Jan Sr. and Jan Jr.
The same household included several working-age sons listed as farmers, showing that this was a large agricultural family migration.
immigration: Listed on the Pieter Floris, departing the Netherlands in April 1847 and arriving at Baltimore before the family continued toward the Pella, Iowa Dutch settlement.
records: Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions

citations.label: WikiTree: Dutch immigrants who settled in Pella, Pieter Floris passenger list
IAGenWeb Marion County: passengers on the Pieter Floris

bio: Sophia Spruit is listed in Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions as Sophia, née Spruit, age 47, housewife, in the household of Gerrit van der Wilt. The household included multiple children and was part of the Dutch immigrant stream associated with the founding generation of Pella, Iowa. Her record is clearest as passenger-list evidence and family context rather than a detailed personal biography.
funFacts: The passenger transcription preserves her maiden name directly as “Nee Spruit.”
Her household traveled with many Dutch farm families, matching the agricultural character of the early Pella colony.
immigration: Listed in 1847 on the Pieter Floris with Gerrit van der Wilt and their children, bound for the Dutch settlement stream that became Pella, Iowa.
records: Pieter Floris passenger-list transcriptions

citations.label: WikiTree: Dutch immigrants who settled in Pella, Pieter Floris passenger list
IAGenWeb Marion County: passengers on the Pieter Floris

bio: Gerritje van de Streek was born at Oldebroek on 2 February 1858, daughter of Helmig van de Streek and Janna Sneller. Her birth record describes her father as a 35-year-old day laborer and her mother as age 34. She later appears in Pella, Iowa cemetery evidence as Gerritje De Wild, buried at Graceland Cemetery with dates 1858–1944.
funFacts: Her Dutch birth record uses the spelling “van de Streek,” while the Iowa cemetery memorial uses the Americanized married form “De Wild.”
The birth registration was made the day after her birth, on 3 February 1858.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record
Find a Grave memorial

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Gerritje van de Streek, 1858
Find a Grave memorial: Gerritje De Wild

bio: Hendrik, memorialized in Iowa as H “Hendrik” De Wild, was born in the Nijkerk area of Gelderland and died on 17 August 1913. His Find a Grave memorial places his burial at Graceland Cemetery in Pella, Marion County, Iowa. The matching cemetery evidence ties the Dutch De Wild line into the Pella Dutch-American community where Gerritje van de Streek De Wild is also buried.
funFacts: His Find a Grave memorial uses only the initial “H” before the quoted given name “Hendrik,” a common pattern for Dutch names in American cemetery indexing.
The memorial gives his birth as 30 November 1856 in Nijkerk Municipality, Gelderland, Netherlands.
records: Find a Grave memorial

citations.label: Find a Grave memorial: H “Hendrik” De Wild
Find a Grave memorial: Gerritje De Wild

bio: Helmig van de Streek is named in his daughter Gerritje’s 1858 Oldebroek birth record as her father. The same civil record gives his age as 35 and his occupation as “daglooner,” a day laborer. This anchors the Van de Streek family in Oldebroek shortly before the later Iowa branch appears in Pella records.
funFacts: The record’s occupational term “daglooner” points to wage labor rather than land ownership or a trade.

A Nationaal Archief emigrant index includes an 1870 Oldebroek entry for a widowed “Helmig-van de Streek” household; because the forename is omitted, it should be treated as migration context, not proof of Helmig himself emigrating.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record for daughter Gerritje
Nationaal Archief/OpenArchieven emigrant index entry

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Gerritje van de Streek, 1858
OpenArchieven/Nationaal Archief: emigrant registration for widow Helmig-van de Streek, 1870

bio: Janna Sneller is named in the Oldebroek birth record of Gerritje van de Streek on 2 February 1858. The record gives Janna’s age as 34 and lists her as having no stated occupation, while Gerritje’s father Helmig van de Streek was recorded as a day laborer. This provides a civil-registration anchor for the Sneller and Van de Streek families before the later Pella, Iowa connection.
funFacts: The birth record preserves Janna’s surname in the Dutch form Sneller before the family’s later American context.
The registration was made in Oldebroek, a Gelderland municipality that appears repeatedly in the Van de Streek/Sneller records.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record for daughter Gerritje

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Gerritje van de Streek, 1858

bio: Hendrika Stomphorst was born at Nijkerkerveen on 27 May 1827, daughter of Hendrik Stomphorst and Trijntje Willems. Her birth record says her father was a day laborer and gives both parents’ ages. In Iowa, a Find a Grave memorial for Hendrika Jan Stomphorst DeWild records her death on 8 December 1916 and burial at Graceland Cemetery in Pella.
funFacts: Her Dutch birth record was registered at Nijkerk on 28 May 1827, the day after her birth.
The Iowa memorial preserves the middle patronymic-style “Jan” in the name Hendrika Jan Stomphorst DeWild.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record
Find a Grave memorial

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Hendrika Stomphorst, 1827
Find a Grave memorial: Hendrika Jan Stomphorst DeWild

bio: Hendrik Stomphorst is identified in the 1827 Nijkerkerveen birth record of his daughter Hendrika. The record gives his age as 35 and occupation as “daghuurder,” a day laborer or hired hand. This civil record connects the Stomphorst family to Nijkerk and provides the parental link into the later De Wild branch.
funFacts: The Dutch occupational word “daghuurder” literally indicates someone hired by the day.
His daughter Hendrika’s birth was registered in Nijkerk even though the event place is Nijkerkerveen.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record for daughter Hendrika

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Hendrika Stomphorst, 1827

bio: Trijntje Willems is named as the mother of Hendrika Stomphorst in an 1827 Nijkerkerveen civil birth record. The record gives Trijntje’s age as 37 and lists no occupation for her, while her husband Hendrik Stomphorst was recorded as a day laborer. This is a compact but reliable civil-registration link for the Stomphorst maternal line.
funFacts: Her record shows the pre-surname patronymic form Willems still functioning as a family identifier in early nineteenth-century Gelderland records.
The same record places the family in Nijkerkerveen, a village within the Nijkerk civil-registration area.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record for daughter Hendrika

citations.label: OpenArchieven/Gelders Archief: birth of Hendrika Stomphorst, 1827

bio: Klaas Cornelis Hakkert’s 1875 Herwijnen death record describes him as a 74-year-old farmer, born in Herwen en Aerdt, and names his parents as Cornelis Hakkert and Hester Verploegh. The same record names Maria van Zee as his partner. A daughter’s 1845 birth record also records Klaas as a 45-year-old farmer in Herwijnen, confirming the family setting used by the later Hackert/De Fockert line.
funFacts: His 1875 death record and daughter Cornelia’s 1845 birth record both call him a “landbouwer,” a farmer.
The records preserve the Dutch Hakkert spelling that later appears beside De Fockert/Hackert forms in Pella family-history materials.
records: Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil death record
Gelders Archief/OpenArchieven civil birth record for daughter Cornelia

bio: Maria van Zee is named as the mother of Kornelia/Cornelia Hakkert in the 1845 Herwijnen birth record for that child. The same birth record lists Klaas Hakkert as the father and describes Maria as having no occupation. Klaas Cornelis Hakkert’s 1875 death record again names Maria van Zee as his partner, tying the Hakkert and Van Zee families together in Herwijnen records.
funFacts: Her daughter’s birth record uses the spelling “Kornelia,” while later family-history entries use Cornelia.
Maria’s name appears in both a birth record and her husband’s death record, giving two independent civil-registration anchors.

bio: The Pella Community Memory Database’s Dutch “Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen” names a section for the children of Leendert de Fockert and Aartje van Maaren. A note on the preceding page says Leendert de Fockert was a “landbouwer en poldermeester,” meaning farmer and polder official. This source ties him to the Herwijnen De Fockert/Hackert family documented in the same register used for the later Pella Hackert line.
funFacts: A poldermeester helped manage local reclaimed-land water works, a very Dutch civic responsibility.
The same PCMD register is a thirty-one-page Dutch family file covering the De Fockert family in Herwijnen from 1601 to 1942 and includes a family crest.
records: Pella Community Memory Database: Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen

bio: Aartje van Maaren is named in the Pella Community Memory Database’s De Fockert register as the wife of Leendert de Fockert and mother in the section “Kinderen van Leendert de Fockert en Aartje van Maaren.” The register pages list their children in Herwijnen, continuing onto the next page with Hendrikus, Jantje, and Klaas Hendrik de Fockert. Through Klaas Hendrik, this Herwijnen family connects to the later Hackert line in Pella.
funFacts: The register’s child list links Aartje directly to Klaas Hendrik de Fockert, whose descendants used the Hackert/De Fockert surname forms.
The PCMD metadata describes the De Fockert register as a Dutch genealogy with a family crest.
records: Pella Community Memory Database: Register of the Family De Fockert in Herwijnen

bio: Stinsen in Friesland documents a Sierck Donia generation at Donia State in Easterein/Oosterend and says one Sierck lived on the state in 1540, sold rents from the Donia estate, and was grietman of Hennaarderadeel from 1550 until his death in 1569. This appears to match the Sierck Siercksz Donia in this tree, though the repeated Sierck names in the Harinxma-Donia line require caution. The same site explains that the Donia house was tied to the prominent medieval Donia family and the Schieringer–Vetkoper conflict known as the Donia War.
funFacts: Donia State was important enough that Stinsen in Friesland says the Doniahuis remained the chief house of Easterein after 1500.
The Donia family gave its name to the Donia War, the 1458–1464 conflict between Schieringers and Vetkopers.
Stinsen in Friesland notes that the state was inherited and occupied through several repeated generations named Sierck, so exact identification should be handled carefully.
records: Stinsen in Friesland article on Donia State at Hidaard/Easterein

bio: AlleFriezen indexes Focke Fransen and Dieucke Auts together in a 14 September 1684 membership/confession entry for the Reformed congregation of Burum and Munnekezijl. Later Kollumerland court guardianship records call Focke Fransen deceased and name Dieucke Auts as mother of their orphaned children Frans, Trijntie/Trijn, and Aut Fockes. These records do not use the Hummersma surname directly, so the Hummersma label should be treated as family-tree context while the Focke-Fransen family relationships are documentary.
funFacts: The 1684 church record says Focke and Dieucke made confession of faith on 14 September 1684.
The 1693 guardianship record names curators for the children after Focke’s death, a glimpse of how orphaned children were legally protected.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven DTB membership record
AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Kollumerland guardianship records

bio: Dieucke Auts appears with Focke Fransen in a 1684 Burum and Munnekezijl Reformed membership record. Kollumerland court records from 1693 and 1704 identify her as mother of Frans, Trijntie/Trijn, and Aut Fockes after Focke Fransen’s death. The records place the family around Burum, Munnekezijl, and Kollumerland in late seventeenth-century Friesland, but they do not themselves use the later Hummersma surname.
funFacts: The 1693 guardianship record calls the children “weeskind,” or orphan children, and gives their ages as 10, 6, and 4.
By the 1704 guardianship entry the same children were recorded as 21, 18, and 16, showing the family’s legal trail over more than a decade.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven DTB membership record
AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Kollumerland guardianship records

bio: Frans Fockes appears in Kollumerland court guardianship records as a child of the deceased Focke Fransen and Dieucke Auts. The 1693 record gives his age as 10, while a 1704 follow-up gives his age as 21, consistent with a birth in the early 1680s. These records do not use the Hummersma surname directly for Frans, so the surname connection should be treated as family-tree context rather than a wording found in the court entries.
funFacts: The guardianship records name his siblings Trijntie/Trijn Fockes and Aut Fockes alongside him.
The age progression from 10 in 1693 to 21 in 1704 is unusually useful for a seventeenth-century Frisian child.
records: AlleFriezen/OpenArchieven Kollumerland guardianship records
